full_response:
Detailed solution

1.  Radial null geodesics in the space-time  
   The metric is  
   ds² = −(1 + r²/ℓ²) dt² + dr² /(1 + r²/ℓ²) + r² dΩ² .  
   For a purely radial light ray dΩ = 0 and ds² = 0, so

          −(1 + r²/ℓ²) dt² + dr² /(1 + r²/ℓ²) = 0
   ⇒      dt = ± dr /(1 + r²/ℓ²) .                                 (1)

   The + sign corresponds to an outgoing ray, the − sign to an ingoing one.

2.  Time to reach the conformal boundary r → ∞  
   Integrate (1) for the outgoing ray that starts at the centre r = 0, t = 0:

          t_∞ = ∫₀^∞ dr /(1 + r²/ℓ²)
               = ℓ ∫₀^∞ d( r/ℓ ) /(1 + (r/ℓ)²)
               = ℓ [ arctan (r/ℓ) ]₀^∞
               = ℓ (π/2) .                                            (2)

   Therefore t_∞ = ℓπ/2 (this was the result of part (a)).

3.  The return to the centre  
   Once the ray has reached r = ∞, eq. (1) with the minus sign brings it back.
   The integral for the ingoing branch is identical, so an additional time t_∞
   elapses before r again equals 0.  Consequently

          r(2 t_∞) = r(πℓ) = 0 .                                     (3)

4.  What arrives at r = 0 at t = 2 t_∞ ?  
   Every photon that left the origin at t = 0 returns exactly at t = 2 t_∞.
   They come back from every direction, so you receive the whole spherical
   wave front you emitted initially.  In other words, you are illuminated by
   your own radiation (light, signals, etc.) that departed at t = 0.

   Since the photons retrace their paths, each one approaches you from the
   opposite direction to which it was emitted; you therefore see an image of
   yourself from the back.  Apart from possible overall redshift/blueshift
   factors (which, for symmetry, cancel for an observer at r = 0), the scene
   is exactly the one you created at the initial instant.

\boxed{\text{At } r=0,\; t = 2t_\infty = \pi \ell \text{ you are hit by all the light you emitted at } t=0;\; you literally see your own back (an image of yourself) arriving in a single flash.}

Confidence: 67%
extracted_answer: \text{At } r=0,\; t = 2t_\infty = \pi \ell \text{ you are hit by all the light you emitted at } t=0;\; you literally see your own back (an image of yourself) arriving in a single flash.
